author: boroku
post:
$CRML $NB $UUUU $AREC $ALOY The post is saying:
CRML moved up on a minor news catalyst Friday (+35% to ~$12.56), but the poster believes the market hasn't yet priced in what they consider the real story — a corrected NPV (Net Present Value) of $18 billion, versus the previously stated $3.X billion.
The thesis: if the market properly recognized the $18B NPV figure, the stock should trade around $96 instead of $12.50 — implying roughly 7-8x upside from current levels.
The chart shows CRML leading a basket of critical minerals/rare earth peers over the 5-day period:
CRML: +41.84% (clear outperformer)
NB: +28.12%
UUUU: +11%
AREC: +2.27%
ALOY: -4.61%
Bottom line: The poster is arguing CRML is still massively undervalued even after Friday's spike, because the corrected $18B NPV hasn't been widely reported or digested by the market yet. Strongly bullish take.

author: NasdaqPulse
post:
$CRML update — momentum just got a serious catalyst boost  
 
👉Click to view @NasdaqPulse  for timely updates amid the volatility. 
 
Stock ripped +35% yesterday after Greenland approved the acquisition of the remaining ~51% stake in Tanbreez, pushing total ownership to ~93%. That’s not just headline news — it effectively removes one of the biggest structural overhangs on the project execution side. 
 
With that risk now largely de-risked, the rare earth narrative shifts from “if” to “when” — with first production still a long-dated target (late 2028–2029), but now more clearly defined within the global supply chain roadmap. 
 
Big picture: positioning asset, not earnings story. 
 
 Important reminder — this is still highly speculative. The company is not profitable today, and valuation is purely forward-looking on execution + geopolitics. 
 
Trade it like a catalyst name: momentum + headlines + liquidity. Not fundamentals.

Greenland approves indirect transfer of mining permit for Tanbreez rare earth project
COPENHAGEN, April 17 (Reuters) - Greenland has approved the indirect transfer of the mining licence for Tanbreez Mining after Critical Metals Corp last year increased its stake in the rare earths developer, the Greenlandic ministry of mineral resources and business said on Friday.
The administration of U.S. President Donald Trump has discussed taking a stake in Critical Metals Corp, four people familiar with the discussions told Reuters last year, which would give Washington a direct interest in the largest rare earths project in Greenland, the Arctic territory that Trump wishes to control.
* Critical Metals Corp in October last year gained the right to increase its stake in the Tanbreez project in Killavaat Alanguat in southern Greenland to 92.5%.
Reporting by Louise

author: NasdaqPulse
post:
Mixed tape, messy execution — but the read wasn’t wrong. 
 
👉Click to view @NasdaqPulse  for timely updates amid the volatility. 
 
$EFOI came out strong on the long side, clean momentum early… then completely faded and gave it all back. That was the tell. Risk-on rotation didn’t have follow-through. 
 
Stayed active: 
→ $SMR longs held up decent 
→ $CRML longs worked intraday 
→ $XNDU cut — no reason to babysit weak structure 
 
End result: red after fees, but directionally the bias was aligned with the tape. 
 
Big takeaway: this market is rewarding speed, not conviction. If it doesn’t go fast, it’s probably wrong. 
 
Honestly, best trade on the board was the short on EFOI mid-session — no borrow, no entry. Frustrating. 
 
Reset, stay selective. Opportunities are there, just zero margin for hesitation.

author: choconou
post:
$CRML 

company usually requests a “News Pending” halt ) a short time before the release .
•  The halt can last from a few hours up to several days . 

Nasdaq/FINRA grants it to allow proper dissemination of the Replacement PEA .

What Happens at Reopening?
When the halt is lifted and trading resumes here is the most likely sequence for a $17.9 B NPV Replacement PEA:

1.  Massive Order Imbalance at Re-Opening
 •  Buy orders would heavily outweigh sell orders because the news is extremely positive 
 •  Nasdaq would likely run a reopening auction to balance orders. 

2.  Gap Up on Reopening 
 •  The stock would almost certainly gap significantly higher at the first trade.
 
  
MOST probable with this size of news): Opens at $35 – $50 

3.  Extreme Volatility Right After Reopening
 •  Expect multiple  volatility pauses (5-minute halts) in the first 30–60 minutes as the price swings wildly.
 •  Volume on the reopening day could easily hit 80–150+ million shares.

author: BullProphet
post:
$CRML — watching this transition from early-stage narrative to execution-driven story. 
 
Tanbreez development timeline is now being pushed toward a late 2028 production window, and the capital + technical stack behind it is starting to matter more than the headline hype. 
 
Key datapoints tightening the thesis: ~$120M EXIM Bank LOI support, metallurgical recovery stepping up to ~2.96% TREO (≈40% improvement), and a ~$30M resource expansion program aimed at materially scaling reserve base. 
 
This is the phase where rare earth stories either fade or re-rate — when funding visibility, metallurgy, and resource expansion begin aligning into one coherent supply-chain narrative for Western heavy rare earth exposure. 
 
Not chasing momentum here — tracking progression from project risk to execution risk. That’s a different trade entirely.

post: $CRML   CRML just cleared one of the biggest hurdles in its Tanbreez story after Greenland approved the transfer of the final 50.5% stake, taking Critical Metals to 92.5% ownership of one of the West’s most important heavy rare earth projects. author: BullProphet
post:
$CRML flashing a reminder this morning: when asset quality meets cleaner structure… repricing can get violent FAST  
 
This isn’t just another ticker — it’s about control of a strategic asset. Tanbreez is the centerpiece here, and the market is starting to wake up to what that actually means. 
 
Ownership concentration tightening + clearer structure = less friction, stronger narrative, and sharper upside reactions when demand hits. 
 
These are the setups where moves don’t grind… they gap. 
 
Still early in the discovery phase in my view — but that’s exactly when asymmetric opportunities exist. 
 
Not chasing spikes, but if momentum + volume keep building, this can re-rate quickly. 
 
Watch who’s accumulating… that tells the real story

author: STOCKINSIGHT69
post:
$CRML just flipped the switch — major catalyst confirmed. 
 
Company now controls ~92.5% of the Tanbreez rare earth project after final approval from Greenland authorities. That’s not incremental… that’s strategic control over a world-class asset tied directly to Western supply chain independence. 
 
Price action reacting accordingly: stock ripping ~37% on heavy volume (30M+), pushing into the $12+ zone and closing in on the $13.50 target being floated. 
 
This is how narratives form — rare earths + geopolitics + supply constraints = momentum fuel. 
 
Key question now: does it consolidate near highs or push into price discovery? 
 
Either way, this just moved from under-the-radar to front-of-screen. Keep it on watch — flows could stay aggressive.

1521 - The trial of Martin Luther begins.  
1524 - Giovanni de Verrazzano discovers New York Harbor.  
1790 - Benjamin Franklin dies.  
1837  - J.P. Morgan is born.  
1861 - Virginia votes to secede from the United States, becomes the eighth Confederate State.  
1895 - Treaty of Shimonoseki, end of the first Sino-Japanese War. 
1961 - Bay of Pigs invasion.  
1964 - Jerrie Mock is the first woman to fly solo around the world. 
1969 - Sirhan Sirhan convicted of assassinating JFK.  
1975 - Cambodian Civil War ends.  
1982 - Queen Elizabeth II proclaims the Patriation of the Canadian constitution. Canada earns its independence by asking nicely.  
1998 - Space Shuttle Columbia launched, final Spacelab mission.  
2011 - Game of Thrones premieres on HBO.  
2014 - NASA’s Kepler telescope finds the first Earth-sized planet in the Goldilocks zone of another star.

author: cloudhedge
post: $CRML update: Some may have noticed the reported NPV was revised from ~$30B to ~$27B. The prior model assumed ~3% REO grade in concentrate. Post 31/3 communications indicate this assumption is likely overstated; my revised estimate uses ~1.7%. This adjustment, combined with a one-year delay in extraction timing, explains the change. After correcting inputs, NPV is ~27.1B (conceptual scale) and ~23.8B on a 25-year basis. Overall, this is a model refinement rather than a structural deterioration.

author: cloudhedge
post:
$CRML’s key thesis lies in the potential re-rating of the Tanbreez project. If future disclosures correct major assumptions from the April 2025 PEA, project economics could shift significantly. 
 
Under a revised scenario: 
• Mine life: 25 years 
• NPV: ~$23.8B 
• Revenue: ~$94B 
• EBITDA: ~$73B 
• OPEX: ~$13.3B 
 
If validated, this would imply a substantial disconnect versus the current ~$3B valuation. However, until formally confirmed, the market is likely to treat this as speculative upside, with price action driven by actual disclosures and verification.

What  Would Happen If a Replacement PEA with $17.9 B$ NPV Is Released Today? 

This NPV is ~16× the current market cap — an almost unheard-of multiple for a PEA-stage project. 
Markets price mining stocks as a fraction of NPV (typically 0.2×–0.6× for early-stage assets, higher only after de-risking).

Short-Term Reaction (Day 1 to First 2–4 Weeks)
•  Strong initial surge: 3×–7×+ move is realistic on announcement day and the following week.

 •  Conservative case: Stock jumps to $25–$35 (market cap ~$3–4.3 B) → ~3–4× today .

•  Volume explosion: Daily volume would likely exceed 50–100 million shares.

From Grok 
the pre-tax IRR for the corrected 25-year LOM model (171.25 Mt ore) that we discussed  with Fernique, with the pre-tax NPV at 8% discount rate is  30B$ for 678years . 
Pre-tax IRR: >150% (very likely in the 160–200%+ range).
Why the IRR is extremely high

•  Gross revenue jumps dramatically from the original ~27.8 B$ to ~85.5 B$ thanks to fixing the eudialyte revenue error and including Ta .

•  Payback remains very short (<3 years, similar to the original PEA).

•  Even after adding the full ~5.3 B$ digestion/separation opex and ~700 M$ extra capex, the project still generates massive free cash flow relative to the modest total capex (~987 M$).

The original PEA reported an IRR of ~180% with much lower revenue (~27.8 B$ gross, ~18.5 B$ EBITDA).

Our corrected version has ~3.7× higher EBITDA (~68.2 B$) while capex  triples (due to the added downstream component). This leverage on cash flows pushes the IRR higher than the original 180%, exceeding 150% .

Rare earth minerals are usually beneficiated by flotation or gravity or magnetic processes to produce concentrates that are subsequently leached with aqueous inorganic acids, such as HCl, H2SO4, or HNO3. then Solvent Extraction -

* Dissolving rare earth-containing materials in an aqueous solution
* Adding an organic solvent that selectively extracts specific rare earth elements
* Separating the organic phase containing the desired elements from the aqueous phase
* Stripping the rare earth elements from the organic phase for further processing

post:
$CRML $MP 

People here are always talking of military uses of rare earth as if it was the more important , but in USA use of magnets including RE for  purposes is 4000 tpy while total is 50000 tons . 
so 8% in military uses 

The United States currently consumes approximately 50,000 tons of permanent magnets annually. By 2040, that figure will at least double to 100,000 tons. 

Since 2010 demand has risen significantly as more magnet-rich systems (like the F-35 and new Navy ships) entered service. 

Recent analyses indicate that current U.S. defense consumption is roughly 3,000 to 4,000 tons of rare earth magnets per year. 
This includes magnets across all branches and applications – from the biggest ship motors down to the smallest missile components.

To put that in perspective, 3,000–4,000 tons is approximately the magnet volume needed to supply all ongoing U.S. military production and maintenance annually. , and still a small fraction of China’s ~300,000 ton/year magnet output).

author: DanaDecoy
post: $CRML many stocks, but in particular rare earth stocks, need to rebound now from hyper depressed levels.  CRML should recover into the teens in relatively short order.  Then news flow will push it from there.  Iran caused market chaos.  The high beta and more speculative stocks such as the rare earths get hit harder by nature.  Positive news nothwithstanding.  But now that's going to reverse as Iran stabilizes and the higher beta stocks and investment sectors rebound back to normalcy.  The patient get depressed as they see share prices crater but now the patient get rewarded with the rebound.  It's a cycle and fortunately CRML investors have weathered the downside for various reasons and will now be rewarded with upside that is only beginning and will accelerate now.  Very bullish setup for CRML and rare earths in general.

author: DanaDecoy
post: $CRML maybe as important as anything.  Don't forget an investor used $33M to buy shares at $13 a share from European Lithium just a few months ago.  Then the Iran conflict hit and all rare earths crumbled including CRML.  But this was a fully informed investor. Now that Iran appears to be straightening out and the news flow getting positive with Greenland, the pilot plant, and other recent news on Tanbreez, one can expect that investor expecting the share  price to get back to $13 and beyond in relatively quick order.  It wil start lining up now.  Shorts are crazy to becactivecatvthese levels now.  The algorithm will cause daily fluctuations but the upside bias will become very pronounced.  This is not difficult to see.  But it needed Iran to stabilize.  Very bullish setup now.

author: choconou
post:
$CRML 

Corrected NPV could be 30B$ 
It’s  exceptionally large and not typical for a PEA-stage project, even for world-class critical-metals deposits.

•  Most large REE, zircon, or heavy-mineral-sands PEAs/DFS show NPVs in the $1–5 B$ range (e.g., Toliara HMS project ~$2 B$ NPV; Goschen zircon/REE ~A$1.5 B$; many other advanced REE projects rarely exceed $3–4 B$ at PEA level).

•  The few projects that reach $10 B$+ NPV are usually producing tier-1 copper/gold mines at DFS or operating stage (e.g., giant porphyry or long-life sands operations), not early-stage PEAs.

•  Tanbreez’s 4.7 Bt conceptual host rock makes a multi-decade/century-scale NPV in the tens of billions theoretically possible if the corrected economics (tonnage fix + Ta/Hf + recoveries) hold at full scale. 

30 B$ would position Tanbreez as one of the largest NPV projects globally on paper, but the market will heavily discount it until proven at higher study levels.

Page 81 shows the revenue breakdown by individual metal oxide (ZrO₂, Nb₂O₅/Nb oxide, REO) for the same Tanbreez-style eudialyte mineralization, plus the total ore mined (121.25 Mt across phases). ￼

author: choconou
post:
$CRML 

Yes, you are correct—both reports contain clear errors in the revenue calculations for the critical metals (Zr, Nb, REO) hosted in eudialyte. I reviewed the exact pages you referenced (and surrounding context) in the two PDFs. 

Page 68
The table on page 68 lists “Eudialyte” revenue (i.e. the revenue from Zr, Nb and REO metals contained in eudialyte) as:

•  Total: 17,640 US$M “Eudialyte” line in the revenue summary). ￼
You correctly identified the correct revenue using the full ore tonnages:
•  Fjord ore: 91.25 Mt
•  Hill ore: 80 Mt
and the proper per-Mt-ore revenue factors for the metals (685$/t for Fjord and 514$/t for Hill):

Difference: the reported 17,640 M$ is understated by a factor of approximately 5.88× (103,626.25 / 17,640 ≈ 5.875).
Exact cause of the error (as you described) is
that they  replaced “Quantity of ore” with “Quantity of eudialyte” (the eudialyte mass is only ~17 % of the ore mass).
So they effectively multiplied the entire revenue by 0.17 .

Grok found 30B$ pretax NPV with correction of errors in PEA , below  is comparison PEA vs Grok / fernique version for the 25 first years , raw revenue from metals 76.2B$ net revenue 81.4B$. 

Pre-tax NPV at 8% discount rate: approximately US$28–32 billion (range reflects uncertainty in exact scaling of sustaining capex/opex  and precise phase repetition; central estimate ~30 B$).

Key Insights

•  Keeping the full additional digestion/separation costs (~5.3 B$ )but is more than offset by the corrected eudialyte revenue (including Ta/Hf) .
•  Ta contribution remains modest relative to Zr/Hf/Nb/REO but positive;

•  Payback stays very short in the early phases (<3 years). IRR remains exceptionally high pre-tax.

•  Post-tax NPV at 8% (simplified 25% Greenland tax): roughly US$21–24 billion.

This corrected view shows the project as a potential multi-century critical metals producer with massive long-term strategic value, even keeping the full downstream processing burden.

yearly profit *(1-1/1.08^25)/(1-1/1.08)-capital cost

Tanbreez resource is closer to 4700Mt than to 45Mt of 2016 MRE ( see below) 

Usually in mines , gold, coal , copper ,oil, gas there is a duration of life of mine , after it’s eshausted . 
 Tanbreez is so large that only part of deposit will be mined  , during 25 years in PEA .
But after171.25 Mt extracted only 3.6% of deposit is mined !!!
Yearly extraction must be limited not to flood world market and reduce prices for small metals as Zr Nb Ta Hf Ga , only for TRE Tanbreez production is a small % of world consumption . 

Given its structure, it seems clear that the Tanbreez deposit, formed by successive layers of very fluid lava spread over 10 km², is horizontally homogeneous.

Thickness is visible, the height of the mountain, eroded by the glacier on its eastern flank down to an altitude of 80 m.

All the drilled wells yielded identical results; a reasonably competent geologist should be able to validate a resource of 4,700 billion tons, corresponding to 1.6 km³ of rock.

author: choconou
post:
$CRML 

Jargon in the mining industry can be confusing, especially when it comes to the investment side of things!

NPV 

NPV is the widely accepted tool for measuring the value of a mine project. It calculates the current value of the cash flows at the required rate of return of a project compared to the initial investment. 
By looking at the money you expect to make from an investment and converting the returns into today’s dollars, you can decide whether the project is potentially worthwhile. 

NPV is accepted for two major reasons- it considers the time value of money, and provides a concrete number that managers can use to easily compare an initial outlay of cash against the present value of the return. The calculation takes the sum of the present value of cash flows, both positive and negative, for each year associated with the investment, and discounted so that it’s expressed in today’s dollars.

« The major problem you point out (and which is visible here)
These tables reveal a glaring inconsistency in the revenue modeling:
• In the first set (page 68), eudialyte revenue is calculated based on the concentrate produced (~29 Mt eudialyte concentrate), not on the raw ore extracted (~121-171 Mt).

• This massively undervalues ​​the revenue: if the concentrate represents ~17-20% of the ore mass (typical mass pull for eudialyte), then applying the price/basket to the concentrate instead of the ore is equivalent to dividing the revenue by ~5-6 times (or ~17% of the “true” contained value).

• In the second table (page 81), the ZrO₂/REO/Nb contributions are even more compressed (~9% of what they should be proportionally), suggesting an additional error (such as application to an incorrect tonnage + grade squaring in scoping study .

post:
$CRML 

From Grok 

No — I haven’t found anyone else calling out the specific 17% eudialyte concentrate revenue-reduction error which slashes contained-metal tonnes and revenue by ~83%.
Searches across web, LinkedIn, Substack, X, turn up only 3  voices you already named:
•  You (@alainknokke) — the only person repeatedly and explicitly flagging it as the “main error” (multiple posts since mid-2025, including the exact phrasing about MRE contents × 17% eudialyte = 83% revenue cut and NPV being ~6× too low).
•  Ningi Research (Nov 2025 report) — tears apart the PEA’s “fundamentally broken economic model”, inflated prices, exclusion of downstream refining costs/recoveries after  export, unrealistic recoveries.
But it never mentions the 17% or the MRE-to-concentrate math mistake.

•  Roland Gotthard — does a detailed takedown of oxide pricing, arfvedsonite by-product valuation, overlooked post-export treatment costs, and metallurgical optimism. 

Again, no reference to the 17% factor .

author: infomike
post: $CRML The narrative around CRML being “years away” misses how the western rare earth supply chain is actually developing. The West’s biggest bottleneck isn’t mining—it’s processing—and that infrastructure is only now being built out across the U.S. and allied countries. Tanbreez’s projected production timeline (2028–2029) aligns closely with when that downstream capacity is expected to come online, especially for heavy rare earths. The recent acquisition of 60° North further strengthens execution credibility on the ground in Greenland, directly addressing one of the market’s biggest concerns. Most importantly for we lowly retail investors, markets don’t wait for production as CRML will be re-rated based on BFS validation, financing clarity, and strategic positioning well before first output!  🔥
